title: practical english usage - Part 169
content:
5 negatives:      auxiliary      + not Do you know where she was going?

Indirect questions (►  260) have the subject before the verb.

Have you seen Andrew? Where was she going? Did Emily phone?

verb - subject - main verb.

Most written questions, and many spoken questions, have the order auxiliary 4 questions:      auxiliary      before     subject For details, ►    H.

She sent the nurse some flowers.   She sent some flowers to the nurse.

direct objects (with a preposition).

Indirect objects can come  before direct objects (without a preposition) or after 3 direct     and   indirect      objects There is a big spider in the bath.

There can be a preparatory subject for an indefinite expression (►  20).

She made it clear that she disagreed.

It's difficult to understand what he wants.

end of a sentence, and use it as a preparatory subject or object (►   268-269).

When  the subject or object is an infinitive phrase or clause, we  often put it at the 2 preparatory         it  and   there pattern, ►    299.

In informal language, especially speech, word order often departs from the basic For sentences that begin with the object (e.g. Those people I can't stand), ►    272.

(e.g. So can I, In came Mrs Parker, ►    270-271).

In a few affirmative structures the verb can come  before the subject The boss has bought another new car.

Anna smiled. Sam is a doctor. My parents Hue in North Wales.

Subject - Verb - (Complement/Object).

The  basic word order of English clauses in positive statements is 1  subject      - verb    - object/complement 215  sentence       structu     re: basic    wo  rd  order    ______ grammar *216   questions: basic rules Did you wash the car today?                       -> not What does 'periphrastic' mean? (     What means 'periphrastic'?) Do you like Mozart? (NOT   L ike you Mozart ?) If there is no other auxiliary verb, we  use do, does or did to form a question.

2  do:   Do   you     like    .. .?

How much  does the room cost? (     How much  the room costs?) looking at?) not What are all those people looking at? (     What all those people are not Why are you laughing? (     Why you are laughing?) ou Have you received my email of June 17? (NOT    Y  have received...?) W  hen is Oliver leaving?  When Oliver is leaving?) In a question, an auxiliary verb normally comes before the subject.

1  auxiliary       verb    before     subject:     Have    you     . . .?

For word order in exclamations (e.g. How kind you are!), ►     223.

For ways of arranging the information in a sentence, ►    267.

What hotel did the President stay in? (informal) In what hotel did the President stay? (formal) structures. For details, ►    209. Compare: In an informal style, a preposition can be separated from its object in certain 8  prepositions:        in   what     .../  what     ...  in For details, ►    Sections 20-24.

If you need help, call me. Call me if you need help.

More than one position is often possible.

Everybody agreed that they were wasting their time.

you do it is your business.

the overall sentence structure.

Other kinds of clause have various positions, depending on their function and The  woman who phoned wanted to speak to the manager.
